title: Inductive Methods for Counting Number Fields
speaker: Brandon Alberts
abstract: We will discuss an inductive approach to determining the asymptotic number of G-extensions of a number field with bounded discriminant, and outline the proof of Malle's conjecture in numerous new cases. This talk will include discussions of several examples demonstrating the method.

proof_stages.label: Counting function and Malle context
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines the counting function for degree-n extensions with prescribed transitive Galois group and bounded discriminant, recalls Malle's predicted shape, and explains the known cases and the subtle failure of Malle's original logarithmic exponent.

proof_stages.label: Normal-subgroup inductive strategy
proof_stages.summary: The proposed method fixes a normal subgroup T of G and seeks to count G-extensions by combining counts for G/T-extensions, information about T-extensions, and class-group torsion or moment bounds over the quotient fields.

proof_stages.label: Fiber decomposition over quotient extensions
proof_stages.summary: The G-counting problem is decomposed by the pushforward map sending a G-extension to the G/T-subextension fixed by T, reducing the global asymptotic to precise asymptotics in each fiber, uniform fiber upper bounds, and a convergence criterion for summing the fibers.

proof_stages.label: Cohomological parametrization of fibers
proof_stages.summary: Each fiber is identified with a set of crossed homomorphisms from the absolute Galois group into T with a discriminant condition, and for abelian T this becomes a twisted Galois-cohomology counting problem with known precise asymptotics.

proof_stages.label: Uniformity through unramified cohomology and class groups
proof_stages.summary: Uniform upper bounds for the cohomological counts are obtained in terms of unramified cohomology divided by a pushforward discriminant, and this unramified term is related to homomorphisms from class groups, producing the class-group moment hypotheses.

proof_stages.label: Assembly of the main theorem
proof_stages.summary: The full theorem states that sufficiently small averaged class-group torsion yields an asymptotic, while weaker bounds still give meaningful upper bounds; in permutation-compatible imprimitive cases the statement can be expressed directly in class-group terms without cohomology.

proof_stages.label: Applications, computations, and scope
proof_stages.summary: The speaker describes application families and computational checks, including many nilpotent and concentrated transitive groups, a complete degree-six table with new asymptotics and weak Malle upper bounds, and ongoing Magma verification for small degrees.

examples.note: The example of the C3 wr C2-type counterexample, with overrepresentation of Q adjoin sqrt(-3), shows why Malle's original logarithmic exponent can fail and why the inductive method must take a maximum over fiber growth rates.
examples.engagement_reason: He identifies Klüners' counterexample as a wreath product and states its overrepresentation mechanism, but does not work through the example quantitatively.

examples.name: Nilpotent transitive groups with abelian minimal-index subgroup
examples.note: This family illustrates a case where the method proves an asymptotic because minimum-index ramification dominates and the relevant class-group bounds are automatically strong enough.
examples.engagement_reason: He develops the nilpotent-family example by defining minimal-index elements, giving the permutation formula for index, and reasoning why those elements dominate the count.

passes.reason: Nilpotent transitive groups satisfying the abelian minimal-index condition are a broad structured family for which the method proves asymptotics.

examples.name: Two-torsion in quadratic class groups from genus theory
examples.note: The formula |Cl(K)[2]| = 2^{number of ramified primes - 1} is used as an analogy for why certain nilpotent class-group contributions are very small.
examples.engagement_reason: He invokes genus theory and states the known formula for two-torsion in quadratic class groups as an analogy/bound, without deriving it.

passes.reason: The genus-theory formula for 2-torsion in quadratic class groups is used as a standard boundedness analogy, not to refute a claim.

examples.engagement_reason: He traces how D4 fields are counted as quadratic extensions over quadratic extensions and how summing the fiber constants gives the infinite sum of L-values.
